title: The role of the denominator
text:
In March 2023 the Home Office published a note correcting its own numbers. The 2021 census had arrived, and the department recalculated stop and search disparity using the new population figures instead of the 2011 ones. The rate at which black people were searched relative to white people fell from 6.2 times to 4.8. No policing had changed. The searches were the same searches. Only the population they were divided by had been updated.
That note is trasparent, but it stops one step short. A rate is a fraction, and almost all public argument concerns the top of it. The bottom gets treated as a fact you look up rather than a choice you make. But you choose which census, whether the population at risk is everyone alive or only the ages the power is used on, and whether to compare people in the same part of the country or pool the lot.
This post holds the numerator completely still: the same seventeen years of recorded searches throughout. Then it changes the denominator four ways. No modelling, no adjustment for anything unobserved. Division.

title: The role of the denominator
text:
In March 2023 the Home Office published a note correcting its own numbers. The 2021 census had arrived, and the department recalculated stop and search disparity using the new population figures instead of the 2011 ones. The rate at which black people were searched relative to white people fell from 6.2 times to 4.8. No policing had changed. The searches were the same searches. Only the population they were divided by had been updated.
That note is trasparent, but it stops one step short. A rate is a fraction, and almost all public argument concerns the top of it. The bottom gets treated as a fact you look up rather than a choice you make. But you choose which census, whether the population at risk is everyone alive or only the ages the power is used on, and whether to compare people in the same part of the country or pool the lot.
This post holds the numerator completely still: the same seventeen years of recorded searches throughout. Then it changes the denominator four ways. No modelling, no adjustment for anything unobserved. Division.

title: The role of the denominator
section: What the pyramids already tell you
text:
What the pyramids already tell you
Before any crime data, the census on its own settles most of what follows. The age pyramids above show two things at once. The minority populations grew far faster than the white population between 2011 and 2021: white up 1%, black 29%, Asian 31%, mixed 40%, other 123%. And they start from much younger age structures. In 2021, 29.3% of the white population was aged 10 to 34, against 39.0% of the black, 39.1% of the Asian and 46.7% of the mixed.
Those two facts predict the results below without any reference to policing, because a denominator change moves a ratio only to the extent that the groups differ in that characteristic.

title: The role of the denominator
section: What changes when you change the base
text:
What changes when you change the base
Census vintage, the correction the Home Office made, multiplies the black ratio by 0.78, Asian by 0.77, mixed by 0.72 and other by 0.45.
Restricting to ages 10 to 34 does as much or more: a further 0.75 for black and Asian, 0.63 for mixed. This is the age gap in the pyramids, arriving in the arithmetic.
Restricting to males does almost nothing to the comparison, though it halves every rate. The ratios move by 0.96 to 1.03, because every group is close to half male. Sex changes the level and leaves the comparison alone. It is the adjustment people most often assume matters.
Geography does more than any of them, and nobody adjusts for it. In 2018/19 the England and Wales black ratio peaked at 9.7. Every one of its five parts was lower: South 8.3, Midlands 6.7, East of England and Wales 5.4, London 4.5, North 3.7. A national figure above all of its components is what happens when one group is concentrated where searching is heaviest.

title: The role of the denominator
text:
Where it matters most
A third of the celebrated fall was the census arriving. The published ratio ran 9.7 down to 4.1. On a fixed 2021 base the same searches give 7.6 down to 4.1. The remaining fall is real; about a third of the improvement is not.
The correction is four times larger in the North than in London. Recomputing 2018/19 on a fixed base cuts the North from 3.7 to 2.2, a fall of 40%, and London from 4.5 to 4.0, a fall of 11%. It bit hardest where minority populations were smallest and grew fastest.
Nine conclusions reverse. In 2022/23, nine area-and-group combinations sit above parity as published and below it once the base is ages 10 to 34. Asian in the North goes 1.20 to 0.83, mixed in the South 1.61 to 0.98, and the England and Wales “other” figure 1.26 to 0.95. Not that the number moves, but that the sentence changes.
The broad groups hide enormous spread. Within the black group: African 2.06, Caribbean 3.46, “other black” 15.44. Within Asian: Chinese 0.17 and Indian 0.51 against 3.18 for “other Asian”. And “white”, the reference against which all of this is measured, is not uniform either: white British 0.94, other white 1.66.
One of those is a numerator problem. 26,788 of the 57,189 searches of black people in 2022/23, about 47%, were recorded as “other black”, a category containing 297,781 people, half a percent of England and Wales. That is not a finding about who gets stopped. It is a finding about which box gets ticked, sitting inside the headline figure everyone quotes.

title: The role of the denominator
text:
Small counts
Ratios for small groups look like every other number on the chart and should not be read the same way.
Roma is the clearest case, and is excluded here entirely. The census counted 100,970 Roma residents in 2021. The stop and search code, introduced the same year, recorded four searches nationally in 2022/23. That is not a rate of 0.04 per thousand. It is a code officers are not using, and dividing it by a real population produces something that looks like a measurement and is not one. Any cell recorded as zero is treated as missing here for the same reason.

title: The role of the denominator
text:
What this does not show
Adjusting for age, sex or region changes the question. “Is the burden of this power equal across groups” and “among people of the same age and sex in the same place, is treatment equal” are both legitimate, and they have different answers. Neither figure is the true one. What can be said is narrower: several claims stated as facts about policing turn out to be statements about which population was used as the base, and the base is rarely reported next to the number.

title: The role of the denominator
text:
Sources

Home Office, Update to stop and search and arrests statistics using 2021 Census estimates, 2 March 2023
Ethnicity facts and figures, Stop and search, and the underlying 2006 to 2023 data file
Home Office, Stop and search: interpreting and describing statistics
ONS, Ethnic group, England and Wales: Census 2021
Census 2011 table DC2101EW of region by age by sex by ethnic group, via nomis

title: Significant Figures
text:
Headlines love a number. “Overrepresented.” “Surging.” “Widening.” The figure gets repeated everywhere and checked almost nowhere.
This site does the checking. Each post takes one claim you’ve probably seen, tracks down the public data behind it, and works through whether it holds up, showing every step so you can reproduce the answer or argue with it.

title: Significant Figures
text:
Start here
Overrepresented compared to whom? Are women and non-white people really overrepresented in higher education? The England and Wales 2021 census settles it.
The role of the denominator Stop and search disparity ratios swing widely depending on which population you divide by, holding the same seventeen years of searches fixed.

title: About
text:
Significant Figures is about checking the numbers behind the headlines. Each post takes one such claim and works through it with public data, showing not just the answer but how it was reached, so you can reproduce or challenge every figure yourself.
Other fact checking public service sources of the highest integrity: BBC podcast More or Less, The Economist Graphic detail section (paywall), Full Fact, Reuters Fact Check. Full location-specific list.
The site is written by me, Maria Boutchkova, Lecturer in Finance at the University of Edinburgh.
Domain name coincidentally identical and not related to the 2017 book Significant Figures: The Lives and Work of Great Mathematicians by British mathematician Ian Stewart, FRS CMath FIMA.

title: Overrepresented compared to whom?
text: Official statistics report that women and most ethnic minority groups are a larger share of university students than of the general population. Women were 56.6% of first-degree undergraduates in 2021/22 (Advance HE). White pupils have the lowest higher-education entry rate of any broad ethnic group (gov.uk ethnicity facts and figures, with more in the House of Commons Library briefing). In the press this becomes one line: women and non-white people are overrepresented in higher education.

title: Overrepresented compared to whom?
text: Official statistics report that women and most ethnic minority groups are a larger share of university students than of the general population. Women were 56.6% of first-degree undergraduates in 2021/22 (Advance HE). White pupils have the lowest higher-education entry rate of any broad ethnic group (gov.uk ethnicity facts and figures, with more in the House of Commons Library briefing). In the press this becomes one line: women and non-white people are overrepresented in higher education.

title: Overrepresented compared to whom?
text:
The question
“Overrepresented” only means something once you finish the sentence: overrepresented compared to whom? That comparison group is the benchmark, and the benchmark decides the answer. Students are young, and the young population is more ethnically diverse than the old. Compare students against the whole population and you can produce “overrepresentation” out of nothing but age. The honest comparison is against people of the same age.

title: Overrepresented compared to whom?
text:
How we check it
We use one free public source: the England and Wales 2021 census. It records both the population and whether each person holds a degree-level qualification (Level 4 or above, which covers a degree and higher). For residents aged 22 to 24, we compare each group’s share of the population with their share of the degree-level holders, region by region. If a group is a larger share of the holders than of the population, they are overrepresented, and the gap says by how much.
Two choices define the comparison group. Age: 22 to 24, because most people on a standard degree have finished by 22 and the band still reflects recent cohorts. Residence: about one in ten residents that age arrived in the UK within the previous five years, one in five in London. Include them and the count covers everyone now living here who holds a degree, wherever they earned it. Exclude them and it covers only people who were already here when the decision to go to university was made. The claim is about British higher education, so the main figures exclude recent arrivals. Where including them would change the answer, we say so.

title: Overrepresented compared to whom?
text:
Getting the data
On the ONS Create a custom dataset tool, choose Usual residents and set Area type to Regions. Two downloads are needed.
First, add Highest level of qualification, Age, Sex and Ethnic group. Set Age to the categories that include 22 to 24 and Ethnic group to 6 categories. This gives the sex panel and the unadjusted ethnicity figures.
Second, repeat with Length of residence in the UK in place of Sex. This gives the same table split by how long each resident has been in the country.
They have to be separate because ONS suppresses cells small enough to identify individuals. Asking for all five variables at once thins the table until half the regions are withheld. Five variables clear where six do not, so sex and residence are collected in turn rather than together.
This covers England and Wales. Scotland and Northern Ireland ran separate censuses and can be incorporated but it is unnecessary for our purposes.

title: Overrepresented compared to whom?
text:
What it shows
The hollow dot is each group’s share of the population, the filled dot their share of degree-level holders, and the gap between them is the over or under-representation.

Gender 22-24 y olds



Women are a larger share of degree-level holders than of the population in every region, by about 5 to 6 percentage points. Men are the exact mirror.
The sex figures come from the first download and so cover all residents. Applying the residence filter moves the women’s gap from +5.8 to +6.1 points, which changes nothing.


Ethnicity 22-24 y olds



Non-white residents are a larger share of degree-level holders than of the population everywhere except London, from 2.8 points in the North to 5.1 in the Midlands, against 4.6 for England and Wales. London is the exception at -1.1.
Counting recent arrivals too adds between 0.6 and 1.2 points, most of it in the Asian group, and moves London from -1.1 to roughly level. No group changes sign.

title: Overrepresented compared to whom?
text:
And across age groups
The same comparison, now with the population share as the line and each age band placed by how far it sits above or below it. The number by each dot is that band’s share of degree holders.


Take 22 to 24 year olds. Non-white Londoners are 47.7% of Level 4 holders against 48.8% of the population, a gap of -1.1: under-representation. Hover over the two younger positive dots to see the exact gaps.
But almost nobody has finished a three-year degree by 21. Only 20.2% of London 20 to 21 year olds hold anything at Level 4, against 56.3% three years later, and most of that 20% is an HNC, HND, foundation degree or higher apprenticeship rather than a first degree. Non-white Londoners lead on those routes, not on degrees.


The gaps for women ease toward the line with age and turn negative in all regions for the over-50s.

title: Overrepresented compared to whom?
text:
What to keep in mind
The census measures who holds a degree, not who is enrolled today, and at 22 to 24 some are still studying.

title: Overrepresented compared to whom?
text:
What we learned
For women, the claim holds without qualification. Women aged 22 to 24 are a larger share of degree-level holders than of the resident population in every region, by roughly 5 to 6 points, and it persists in weaker form in older age bands.
For ethnicity, it holds in direction but not uniformly. Among residents already here five years before the census, non-white people aged 22 to 24 are overrepresented everywhere except London, where they are slightly underrepresented. The gaps run 2.8 to 5.1 points and narrow in older cohorts. Recent arrivals lift these figures, but by less than one might worry, and in London by enough to close the gap.

Reproducible from public data. The tables are built from the 2021 Census of England and Wales through the ONS Create a custom dataset tool, using the selections above.
Doing it yourself. You do not need to be a programmer. With a current AI model (as of mid-2026) as your guide, install WSL on Windows, then Miniconda, Python and a few libraries; the model gives you the exact commands and fixes anything that breaks. Download the table from ONS, hand it to the model, and ask for a script that draws any visualization you want. On a Mac or Linux machine most of this is already in place.
